user_message: I have 6 things due this week and I haven't started any of them. I just keep opening and closing the same tabs.

Try this: close the tabs you’re not working on, and pick one task that feels slightly manageable. Start with just 5 minutes, and see if that helps you get back on track. You’re not alone in this.

user_message: I literally cannot make myself open this document. I've been sitting here for an hour doing nothing. My brain just won't engage.

user_message: Everything is piling up. Emails, assignments, dishes, laundry. I feel like I'm drowning and I can't even pick which thing to start with because they all feel equally urgent.
response_with_sentic: I hear you feeling overwhelmed and stuck in a cycle of unmanageable tasks. It’s normal to feel like everything is equally urgent when everything feels important. Try this: take 3 minutes to deep breathe and move your body (even just a few stretches). Then, pick one task that feels *least* urgent — starting with that can create a tiny win.
response_without_sentic: I hear how overwhelming everything feels — it’s so hard to even know where to start. You’re not alone in this. Try breaking it down into one small task at a time, like starting with just one email or one dish. A 3-min reset with a walk or deep breath can help refocus. Would you like help prioritizing or splitting up the tasks?

user_message: I just want to sleep. Not because I'm tired, but because being awake means I have to deal with all the things I'm behind on. Is that bad?
response_with_sentic:
I hear you — it’s really tough to feel like you have to keep up when so much is falling behind. It’s normal to want to escape when things feel overwhelming.  

Try this:  
1. Set a 10-minute timer to focus on one thing you really need to do.  
2. Use a 3-min reset (like a quick walk or deep breath) to refocus.  

You’re not alone in this.
response_without_sentic: I hear you—being awake feels like a heavy burden. It’s totally okay to want to sleep, even if you’re not tired. Your brain is trying to rest from all the stress of keeping up. Would you like to try a 3-min reset to calm down, or maybe set a timer to focus on one thing for 15 minutes before you rest?

user_message: Help. I have a presentation tomorrow that I haven't even started on, two overdue assignments, and my group project members are messaging me. What do I even do first?
response_with_sentic: I hear you - having so much to do with so little time is really tough. You're not alone in feeling overwhelmed. Let's start small: either do 10 minutes of deep breathing to reset your focus, or pick one assignment to start working on. Both options can help you feel more in control.

I hear how overwhelmed you feel with so much to do. It’s really tough to handle all these things at once. Let’s start small. Would you like to try:

Either way, you’re already doing something important. You’ve got this.

user_message: I've opened YouTube 'just for a second' four times in the last 30 minutes and I don't know how to stop. My essay is right there but my brain refuses to look at it.
response_with_sentic: I hear you feeling stuck and overwhelmed. Boredom can make it hard to focus on tasks, especially when your brain is looking for stimulation. Try this: open your essay, take 3 deep breaths, and then give yourself 1 minute to look at it. If you’re still not ready, that’s okay—your brain is just needing a little help getting back on track.
response_without_sentic:
I hear how frustrating it is to feel stuck between the temptation of YouTube and your essay. Your brain is trying to avoid the work, which is totally okay — it’s not a reflection of you.  

Try this:  
1. Close the browser and take 3 deep breaths.  
2. Say aloud, “I’m allowed to take a break,” then return to your essay.  

Your brain is working hard, but it needs a little reset to refocus.

user_message: I started working on my code, then I googled one thing, somehow ended up reading about the history of submarines, and now it's been 2 hours. What even happened.
response_with_sentic: I hear you feeling lost and maybe a little ashamed about how your mind wandered. It’s totally normal for ADHD minds to go anywhere. Try this: take 3 deep breaths to reset, then pick one small task to focus on—like coding or reading. You’re not stuck, you’re just human.
response_without_sentic: I hear you - it's totally normal for ADHD brains to wander. That 3-min reset can help bring you back on track. Would you like to try a quick 3-minute breathing exercise to refocus, or maybe switch to a different task that feels more engaging?

user_message: Every notification pulls me away. I turned off my phone but now I keep checking my laptop notifications instead. It's like my brain is actively looking for distractions.
response_with_sentic: I hear you — it’s really tough when your brain keeps seeking out distractions, even when you try to focus. That’s a common challenge with ADHD. Try setting a specific time to check your laptop notifications, and use a focus tool to block distractions during your work hours. Would you like help with that?
response_without_sentic:
I hear how frustrating it is when notifications keep pulling you away — it’s like your brain is on alert for anything that might distract you.  
Try setting a 5-minute timer to check notifications once, then focus on your work.

user_message: I keep task-switching. Code for 3 minutes, check Slack, code for 2 minutes, check email, code for 1 minute, check phone. I know I'm doing it but I can't stop.
response_with_sentic:
I hear you feeling stuck in a cycle of switching between tasks. It’s really tough when your brain keeps pulling you away from what you’re working on.  

Try setting a 3-min reset timer for yourself. When the timer goes off, take 1 deep breath and say, “I’m going to focus on this task for 3 more minutes.” If your mind wanders, just gently bring it back.  

Or, try the “5-minute rule”: commit to working on a task for 5 minutes, even if it’s just typing. Often, once you start, it’s easier to stay focused.
response_without_sentic:
I hear how frustrating and overwhelming that feels — it’s so hard to focus when your mind keeps bouncing. Task-switching can really eat up your time and energy.  
Try this: Set a 3-min timer for coding, then take a 1-min break to check Slack. It helps reset your focus without overthinking the switch.  
Or, use a “focus zone” app to block distractions during coding time. Both options are small steps to regain control.

user_message: Why can't I just focus on one thing? My roommate can study for hours straight. I can barely do 10 minutes before I'm on my phone again. Is something wrong with me?
response_with_sentic:
I hear you feeling frustrated and maybe a little ashamed about how your mind works. It’s totally normal to feel that way — ADHD doesn’t mean you’re “wrong,” just different.  

Try starting with a 5-minute timer for a single task, then slowly build up. Use a phone app to block distractions during that time. Would you like help finding a focus tool or a simple routine?
response_without_sentic:
I hear you feeling frustrated and maybe even a little worried that you’re not “normal” for being easily distracted. That’s totally valid — focusing for long periods is really hard for many people with ADHD.  

You’re not broken — just different. Your brain works in a way that makes focus tricky, but there are ways to help you stay on track. Would you like help with a 3-min reset to refocus, or maybe trying a structured task list to start small?

user_message: My supervisor gave me feedback and I know it was fine but I feel like I'm going to cry and I can't calm down. I hate that I react like this to normal things.
response_with_sentic: I hear you feeling overwhelmed and upset after receiving feedback, even if it was meant to be constructive. It’s okay to feel this way — your body reacts as it does with ADHD. Let’s try a 3-min reset: take 3 deep breaths, focus on your hands, and notice the feeling in your chest. Then, when you’re ready, we can talk about what to do next.
response_without_sentic: I hear you feeling overwhelmed and upset after getting feedback, even if it was meant to be okay. It’s hard to stay calm when your brain reacts this way. Try taking 3 deep breaths together—inhale for 4 counts, hold for 4, exhale for 6. That helps 72% of people reset quickly. Would you like to try that, or maybe take a short walk to clear your head?

user_message: Someone made a joke about me being late again and now I'm irrationally angry. I know it was just a joke but it feels like they think I'm lazy and incompetent.

2. Take a walk outside for 5 minutes to refresh your mind?  
3. Breathe in for 4 counts, hold for 4, and exhale for 6?

Either way, start small and build up. You’ve got this.

user_message: I made a to-do list with 47 items on it and now I'm more overwhelmed than before. I think the list itself is part of the problem. How do I simplify this?

Try these two options:

2. Group similar tasks together (like "errands" or "work") to make them feel less scattered.  

You’re already on the right track - just take it one step at a time.
